stories.headline: Mount Blue Sky to Reopen May 22 After Two Years of Road Repairs; Reservations Now Open
stories.summary:
Mount Blue Sky Recreation Area will reopen to the public on May 22 for the first time since Labor Day 2024, when the highest paved road in the United States was closed to address severe buckling and water drainage issues. Denver Parks and Recreation, which now manages the site locally, announced that reservations are required for visits to the three developed areas: Mount Goliath Natural Area, Summit Lake Park, and the Mount Blue Sky Summit.

Reservations can be made up to 30 days in advance through recreation.gov. An all-sites pass costs $20, motorcycle reservations are $15, and bicycle access is free. Because management has shifted to Denver Parks and Recreation, federal lands recreation passes will no longer be accepted.

To broaden access, free passes will be available through Clear Creek County and Denver Public Libraries. Reservation fees will also be waived on Aug. 1 for Colorado Day and for enrolled tribal members.

stories.headline: New Research Warns Colorado Is Losing Businesses at One of the Worst Rates in the Nation
stories.summary:
A report from the Greenwood Village-based Common Sense Institute found that Colorado ranked 48th out of 50 states in net business establishment births per thousand residents in 2024, with a negative 0.67 rate. The state also ranked last in openings of physical places of business, at a negative 2.25 establishments per thousand residents, worse than Oregon and Massachusetts.

The CSI report drew on U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics Business Employment Dynamics data and a separate Colorado Chamber of Commerce study that documented a net loss of 98 firms and 13,607 jobs from the state since 2019. Researchers said the findings indicate high costs of doing business in Colorado and low confidence among business owners in the state's economic future.

The findings conflict with state data showing an increase in new business filings, a discrepancy CSI attributes to timing differences and differing datasets. Colorado's labor force also shrank, with the state's participation rate falling to 66 percent in March — the lowest since 2020 — even as non-farm payroll jobs grew by 1,400 and unemployment held steady at 3.9 percent.

stories.headline: Colorado Legislature Weighs Bill to Counter Natural Gas Ballot Initiative; Multiple Measures Advance in Final Session Days
stories.summary:
Democratic lawmakers at the Colorado Capitol are drafting legislation aimed at blunting a proposed ballot initiative that would give residents the constitutional right to buy and sell natural gas. Initiative 177 is a two-sentence constitutional amendment that backers say would protect consumer access to natural gas for cooking and heating; legislators said their bill would add definitions and guardrails to address what they called poorly defined and potentially dangerous implications.

Separately, the state Senate passed House Bill 1210 on a 19-15 vote, sending a ban on 'surveillance pricing' — the practice of using consumer data to set individualized prices on groceries, plane tickets, electronics, and other goods — to Gov. Jared Polis. Polis has greeted similar legislation with skepticism in the past, and his office has not committed to signing the measure.

Also this week, a bill that would have allowed the state to invest enterprise fees and public money in privately managed investment funds died in committee over constitutional concerns, and a bill requiring legislative caucuses to disclose their donors was voted down, with one Democratic senator joining Republicans to kill it amid ongoing ethics investigations into lawmakers who attended a privately funded retreat.

stories.headline: Montrose Police Officers Shoot, Injure Armed Man Following 911 Call About Rifle
stories.summary:
Two Montrose Police Department officers shot and injured an armed man Saturday afternoon after responding to a 911 call about a person carrying a rifle in the 100 block of Spruce Drive, the Colorado Bureau of Investigation announced. Officers encountered the man at approximately 12:29 p.m. and fired their weapons. No officers were injured in the confrontation.

The man was transported to a hospital and is reported to be in stable condition following surgery. The shooting is being investigated by the Seventh Judicial District Critical Incident Team, which handles officer-involved shooting investigations in the region.

Anyone with information about the incident is asked to contact WestCO Dispatch at 970-249-9110.

stories.headline: Update: Avalanche Lose Game 3 to Wild 5-1, Series Now 2-1 as Playoffs Return to Minnesota
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche suffered their first loss of the 2026 playoffs Saturday night, falling 5-1 to the Minnesota Wild in Game 3 at Grand Casino Arena in St. Paul. The Wild were described as the clearly dominant team from the opening puck drop, cutting Colorado's series lead to 2-1.

Goaltender Scott Wedgewood was pulled during the game as Minnesota's offense overwhelmed the Avalanche's defense. The loss marks the first time Colorado has trailed on the scoreboard in this postseason run after winning the first two games convincingly.

The series now shifts with new urgency as the Avalanche look to regroup before Game 4. Colorado still leads the second-round series 2-1.

stories.headline: Nuggets Hold End-of-Season Press Conference; Adelman Returns, Murray's Role Questioned
stories.summary:
Denver Nuggets president Josh Kroenke, executive vice president of basketball operations Ben Tenzer, and head coach David Adelman held an end-of-season press conference Friday at Ball Arena, confirming Adelman will return for a second season after the team's first-round playoff exit. Kroenke credited Adelman for keeping the team competitive during Nikola Jokic's knee injury earlier in the season, saying the coaching staff 'really shined' during that stretch.

Front office executives acknowledged the need to address the team's offensive structure, with Jamal Murray's effectiveness in the playoffs increasingly scrutinized after opponents successfully pressured him with physical defenders. Kroenke described the past season as 'the season that never was,' citing an unusual volume of injuries to Jokic, Aaron Gordon, Peyton Watson, and Cam Johnson.

Adelman said he intends to incorporate criticism from trusted sources as he enters his second year as a first-time NBA head coach. Key roster decisions, including the future of players such as Watson and Gordon, remain unresolved heading into a pivotal offseason.

stories.headline: Sean Payton Says Bo Nix Will Be 'Full Speed' by Training Camp After Follow-Up Ankle Surgery
stories.summary:
Denver Broncos head coach Sean Payton said Saturday that quarterback Bo Nix will be fully healthy before the start of training camp in late July, despite undergoing a second procedure on his right ankle in late April. Payton described the follow-up surgery as a planned staged procedure and said it was one of six such two-step operations the team managed this offseason.

Nix is expected to be held back during the early portions of the offseason program, with backup Jarrett Stidham taking the majority of snaps. Payton indicated Nix may participate in some capacity during mandatory minicamp, which runs June 16-18, though the team will be conservative with his workload.

Sources told The Denver Post the follow-up procedure was likely going to be necessary at some point — either now or after the 2026 season — and that Nix and the team chose the current window to avoid the procedure hanging over the upcoming year. Nix originally fractured his ankle in overtime of Denver's AFC Divisional Round win over Buffalo.

stories.headline: Update: Passengers Recount Terror Aboard Frontier Flight After Plane Struck and Killed Person on Denver Airport Runway
stories.summary:
Passengers aboard the Frontier Airlines Airbus A321 that struck and killed a person on a Denver International Airport runway are speaking publicly about the harrowing moments during and after the incident. Witnesses described hearing a loud boom during takeoff, seeing flames and sparks from the engine, and smoke filling the cabin as the pilots aborted the flight.

'I thought I was going to die,' said passenger Mohamed Hassan, who chose not to rebook on the replacement flight. Others described being told to remain seated while the engine was visibly on fire, a period they called disorienting and terrifying. Passenger Nikil Thalanki said the plane's wheels had briefly left the ground before it returned to the runway.

Frontier confirmed the Airbus A321 was carrying 224 passengers and seven crew members. Denver firefighters extinguished a brief engine fire, and passengers were eventually transported to their destination, Los Angeles International Airport, on a separate aircraft. The incident remains under investigation.

stories.headline: Sakura Square Renovation Underway as Denver's Japanese-American Cultural Hub Eyes Larger Redevelopment
stories.summary:
Scaffolding has gone up around parts of Sakura Square, long considered the heart of Denver's Japanese-American community, as leaders work to repair aging buildings near 20th Street and Lawrence Street in downtown Denver. The repair work is part of a broader effort that could lead to a larger redevelopment of the entire block.

The square has served as a cultural and community anchor for Denver's Japanese-American population for decades. Organizers are balancing the need for structural repairs with plans to preserve the site's cultural identity through any future redevelopment.

stories.headline: Denver Area Gardening Season Kicks Off with Plant Sales, Mother's Day Weekend Timing
stories.summary:
Thousands of Denver-area residents turned out for annual plant sales at the Denver Botanic Gardens and Denver Urban Gardens on Friday and Saturday, marking what many consider the unofficial start of the gardening season in Colorado. Vendors offered a wide selection of herbs, vegetables, flowers, hanging baskets, and drought-tolerant plants ahead of Mother's Day weekend.

Candace Wickstrom, manager at City Floral Garden Center in Denver, said Mother's Day weekend traditionally signals the beginning of planting season in the region, even as the state's unpredictable spring weather — including a recent significant snowstorm — keeps gardeners cautious. Temperatures are forecast to reach the low 70s for the holiday weekend before climbing into summer-like heat next week.

stories.headline: Arapahoe County Sheriff Warns Residents of Bobcat Roaming South Denver Metro Area
stories.summary:
The Arapahoe County Sheriff's Office is asking residents in the south Denver metro area to remain alert after reports of a bobcat moving through the area. Officials have not specified the exact location of the sightings but are urging people to keep a safe distance from the animal and to secure pets and small livestock.

Bobcat encounters in suburban Colorado are not uncommon during spring, when wildlife becomes more active and younger animals may range into populated areas. Residents are advised to contact local wildlife authorities if they observe the animal.

stories.headline: Film on the Rocks Announces 2026 Season at Red Rocks with 'Sinners,' 'The Princess Bride,' and 'Jurassic Park'
stories.summary:
Film on the Rocks has revealed its 2026 season lineup at Red Rocks Amphitheatre, featuring screenings of 'Sinners,' 'The Princess Bride,' and 'Jurassic Park.' The beloved outdoor film series draws large crowds each summer to the iconic Morrison venue.

The announcement comes as Red Rocks also prepares to welcome visitors via the new free Westracks shuttle connecting RTD's Golden light rail stop to the amphitheater. Specific screening dates and ticketing details are expected to be released in the coming weeks.

joke.joke: You know you're getting old when you get that one candle on the cake. It's like, 'See if you can blow this out.'

topStory.headline: U.S. and Iran Exchange Fire Near Strait of Hormuz, Testing Fragile Ceasefire
topStory.summary:
The United States and Iran exchanged fire near the Strait of Hormuz on Thursday, escalating tensions in the region and casting doubt on the stability of an existing ceasefire. President Donald Trump said on Truth Social that the exchanges occurred as three U.S. military destroyers transited the strait, and that Iranian attackers and numerous small boats were destroyed, with no damage to American vessels. A cargo ship was also reported on fire off Qatar's coast after being struck in the area.

On Friday, U.S. Central Command said it had fired on two additional tankers it alleged were violating its blockade of Iranian ports. The United Arab Emirates, a key Gulf ally of the United States, separately said it was responding to a missile and drone attack. Secretary of State Marco Rubio told reporters Friday that the U.S. was awaiting a response from Iran, expressing hope that any reply could lead to "a serious process of negotiation."

The Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly 20 percent of the world's oil supply previously transited before the U.S. and Israel struck Iran on Feb. 28, has seen no ship traffic for two consecutive days, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence. Trump told NBC News on Friday that the U.S. effectively controls the waterway, adding that no vessels are entering Iranian ports and that Iran is under severe economic pressure from the blockade.

In the NBC News interview, Trump said the conflict was not over, stating, "It's over when it's over," while noting that negotiations over Iran's nuclear program are ongoing and that a deal is close on multiple issues. He also raised the prospect of more drastic action, saying, "There is another concept: Demolish the country and keep the oil," though he suggested he did not believe American public opinion would support such a move. The remarks underscored the uncertain trajectory of both military operations and diplomatic efforts in the region.
